shortDescription: The Macabre Talisman of Thieves and Dark Magic
longDescription:
The Macabre Talisman of Thieves and Dark Magic The Hand of Glory (from French "main de gloire" or corrupted from "mandragore"/mandrake)
                    is one of the most infamous magical objects in European occult tradition. This gruesome
                    artifact consists of the severed, pickled, and dried hand of an executed criminal, typically
                    one hanged at a crossroads, fashioned into a candleholder or itself used as a candle.

extendedContent.title: Description and Nature
extendedContent.content:
The Hand of Glory (from French "main de gloire" or corrupted from "mandragore"/mandrake)
                    is one of the most infamous magical objects in European occult tradition. This gruesome
                    artifact consists of the severed, pickled, and dried hand of an executed criminal, typically
                    one hanged at a crossroads, fashioned into a candleholder or itself used as a candle.
                
                
                    According to folklore, when the fingers were lit (or when a candle made from the criminal's
                    fat was placed in the hand), the Hand of Glory possessed the power to paralyze all who
                    beheld its light and to open any lock it touched. This made it the legendary tool of
                    burglars and thieves throughout medieval and early modern Europe.

Traditional Preparation
                
                    The grimoire traditions provide detailed (and gruesome) instructions for creating a Hand
                    of Glory. The hand had to be severed from a hanged criminal while still on the gibbet,
                    preferably during an eclipse or at a crossroads. It was then wrapped in a piece of the
                    corpse's shroud, squeezed to drain remaining blood, and pickled in an earthen vessel
                    with various salts and peppers for a fortnight.
                
                
                    After pickling, the hand was dried in an oven with vervain and fern, then fashioned
                    into a candle holder. A candle was made from the criminal's fat, virgin wax, and
                    sesame oil (or alternatively, the fingers themselves were set alight). The resulting
                    artifact was believed to be virtually unstoppable in its magical effects.
                

                Countermeasures
                
                    Folk tradition also preserved methods to defend against the Hand of Glory:
                
                
                    Anointing the threshold with an ointment made from the gall of a black cat, owl fat, and blood
                    Blood from a screech owl was said to neutralize its effects
                    Milk was believed to be the only substance that could extinguish its flames
                    Keeping consecrated objects or religious items in the home
extendedContent.title: Creation and Ritual

extendedContent.content:
Several alleged Hands of Glory have survived as museum curiosities, most notably the
                    specimen at the Whitby Museum in North Yorkshire, England. This dried hand, discovered
                    in a cottage wall in the early 20th century, dates to approximately the 18th century
                    and represents one of the few physical examples of this legend made real.
                
                
                    Court records from the 17th and 18th centuries occasionally mention criminals found
                    in possession of "mummified hands" or similar items, suggesting that some individuals
                    genuinely believed in and attempted to create these talismans for criminal purposes.
extendedContent.title: Historical Examples

sources.name: Albertus Magnus, *Compendium Theologicae Veritatis*
sources.description: A medieval theological compendium attributed to Albertus Magnus, containing sections dealing with natural magic and occult practices.
sources.title: Albertus Magnus, *Compendium Theologicae Veritatis*

sources.name: Francis Grose, *A Classical Dictionary of the Vulgar Tongue*
sources.description: An 18th-century dictionary of slang and colloquialisms, including entries on criminal terminology and folklore.
sources.title: Francis Grose, *A Classical Dictionary of the Vulgar Tongue*

sources.name: Reverend Richard Harris Barham, *The Ingoldsby Legends*
sources.description: A collection of humorous and macabre stories and poems published in the 19th century, often drawing on folklore and legend.
sources.title: Reverend Richard Harris Barham, *The Ingoldsby Legends*

sources.name: Sabine Baring-Gould, *Curious Myths of the Middle Ages*
sources.description: A 19th-century study of various myths and legends prevalent in medieval Europe, exploring their origins and cultural significance.
sources.title: Sabine Baring-Gould, *Curious Myths of the Middle Ages*

sources.name: Folklore Collections (Various)
sources.description: Numerous collections of folklore compiled throughout the 19th and 20th centuries, documenting regional beliefs and practices related to magic and the supernatural.
sources.title: Folklore Collections (Various)

description:
The Macabre Talisman of Thieves and Dark Magic The Hand of Glory (from French "main de gloire" or corrupted from "mandragore"/mandrake)
                    is one of the most infamous magical objects

keyMyths.title: The Perpetual Light
keyMyths.description: The Hand of Glory, when properly prepared and wielding a candle crafted from specific ingredients (often human fat), could emit a light that only illuminated the holder while plunging all others into darkness. This allowed thieves to operate undetected.

keyMyths.title: The Unlocking Touch
keyMyths.description: Touching a door or lock with the Hand of Glory was said to render it open, or at least paralyze anyone attempting to guard it. This made it an invaluable tool for burglars seeking entry into fortified places.
keyMyths.source: Francis Grose, *A Classical Dictionary of the Vulgar Tongue*, 1785

keyMyths.title: The Sleeping Draught
keyMyths.description: Some accounts claim the Hand of Glory possessed the power to induce deep slumber in all those within its vicinity, rendering victims completely helpless and unaware of any intrusion.
keyMyths.source: Reverend Richard Harris Barham, *The Ingoldsby Legends*, 1840

keyMyths.title: The Stolen Silver Tankard
keyMyths.description: A popular tale involves thieves using the Hand of Glory to enter an inn, paralyzing the occupants. They are thwarted when a clever serving girl, immune to the Hand's power due to her quick thinking, throws water on it, extinguishing the light and breaking the spell.
keyMyths.source: Sabine Baring-Gould, *Curious Myths of the Middle Ages*, 1866

keyMyths.title: The Fatal Flame
keyMyths.description: If the candle in the Hand of Glory went out, it was believed to signify that one of the thieves was about to be apprehended, or that the ritual had been improperly performed, bringing misfortune upon the perpetrators.
